text: The video demonstrates the placement of four laparoscopic ports in a standard configuration for a cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon applies fundal traction towards the right shoulder to open the Calot's triangle, while inferolateral traction at the infundibulum is used to identify the common bile duct, Rouviere's sulcus, and the right hepatic pedicle.

The surgeon begins dissection above the level of the Rouvière's sulcus, focusing on the posterior aspect of the gallbladder. The peritoneum is superficially dissected up to one-third of the gallbladder's body using a Maryland dissector, with care taken to avoid large structures such as vessels.

The surgeon continues dissection over the anterior aspect of the gallbladder, carefully isolating the cystic lymph node from the cystic artery. The cystic artery is typically located posterior to the lymph node.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform blunt dissection, skeletonizing the cystic duct and creating a window between the cystic duct and artery. The complete length of the cystic duct is manipulated to remove any remnant stones by milking them towards the gallbladder.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, then clips and sections the cystic artery and duct using surgical scissors.

The surgeon continues to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, starting with anterior dissection up to the fundus and then proceeding posteriorly. A small bile leak is observed at the infundibulum traction point, which is clipped to prevent spillage. The area is irrigated with normal saline.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection between the cystic plate and the gallbladder, revealing a clear avascular plane that separates these structures.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder without perforating it or injuring the cystic plate or liver parenchyma. After complete removal, hemostasis is achieved in the liver bed, and the gallbladder specimen is placed into an endobag for retrieval.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical field and liver bed with copious amounts of normal saline. The pneumoperitoneum is then completely suctioned out from the surface of the liver.
